title: Like an large consultancy.
pros: Fair pay and autonomy. Flexible work schedule and location. Lots of opportunities if you are to tap into the right channels and willing to relocate.
cons: Everything is self-serve, from HR, to IT, to career growth. Poor management structure and performance review process. Standard consultancy moonlighting as a large corporation. If you roll of a project your time with the company is likely limited if you don't find another project quickly. Lots of pressure to hire and build teams with junior and offshore resource so don't expect to be working with top tier talent.

title: Wish I would've moved on sooner
body: Wish I would've moved on sooner
pros: Met some really nice people. Most people that started out together were the same age and there was a sense of community.
cons: Zero to not a lot of room for growth. Zero culture and/or values. Pays well below market rate. Hard to make an impact. Disrespectful and outright unprofessional managers. No structure or systems in place, everything is constantly being improvised. People don't have all of the resources needed to succeed. I seriously wouldn't recommend it to anyone. I spent several years there, and it was one of the biggest mistakes of my life.

title: Run.
pros: Working at Capgemini pays the bills until the next thing comes along. CG is a decent employer to kickstart or end a career. Mid-Career you dont want to be constantly re-interviewing and re-selling yourself. In some geographies (like India) there is promotion based on time in position. That won't happen in the US.
cons: Constant need to re-sell oneself and re-interview. High layoff risk. Raises are either very low or skipped. I know a lot of people hanging around on year old promises that didn't materialize. There is a strange people manager culture of disengaged "people managers" who write performance reviews with no work quality insights.

pros: Open culture Easy to access leadership Diversified Ethical Fast-Paced Environment: The work environment is dynamic and fast-paced, which can be intense but also keeps things exciting and prevents monotony. Continuous Learning: Employees are constantly encouraged to learn new skills and adapt to changes, which can be demanding but also ensures personal and professional development.
cons: The company promotes a competitive culture, which can be tough but also motivates employees to perform at their best and achieve their goals.

title: This company is a double standard, be aware
body: This company is a double standard, be aware
pros: Lots of training, sometimes unnecessarily.
cons: - Lots of training, this is due to the company's double standard, while they promote training for growth, leadership only cares about their number, you don't count. - Lack of opportunities: Leadership claims to be open and diverse, but it's the opposite. I saw diverse leaders repeatedly displaced and replaced by non-diverse resources. - Lack of real career opportunities. It's pretty challenging to navigate the company, and there is a division between the America's operation, and European operations

When the project was completed because the client wanted something different or looking for someone else, Capgemini did nothing to find a new client! Bench is the solution. title: Disappointing Hike Policies, Limited Growth and HR Inefficiencies
body: Disappointing Hike Policies, Limited Growth and HR Inefficiencies
pros: Receive appreciation emails, but without salary increases, though they are fortunate to have onsite work opportunities.
cons: Career Opportunities: Limited growth, unclear career paths, and minimal internal mobility. Compensations and Benefits: compensation and benefits fail to meet industry standards. Culture and Values: lacking transparency, management preaches values but fails to practice them. Diversity and Inclusion: lack of diversity and support for marginalized, homogeneous workforce. Senior Management: senior management makes decisions disregarding team impact. Work / Life Balance: Expectation of long hours and constant availability leads to burnout.

title: Good but be careful
pros: Good place to start career with a solid company name to put on your resume also. In theory decent structure. Good young environment in office. Lot of good people. Lot of opportunities to learn. Alot of your co-workers want to help you. Work/life balance is pretty solid especially relative to other companies. Even if you get stuck with a tougher client its still really not too bad relative to other companies.
cons: Alot of the organization is better in theory/on paper than the actual reality of it. Constant shuffling of hierarchy. Ceiling on how far you can rise in the company, the best employees I know all were gone within 2 years of joining. Everything comes down to avoiding the bench at all costs for any extended period of time even if they will disingenuously tell you "we really try to avoid at all costs laying off Grade A employees" or "it takes being on the bench for close to a year to be vulnerable to lay-offs". Alot of their problems stem from way overhiring a few years ago, not knowing what to do when the tech bubble burst, and they are still feeling the effects of that 18-24 months later.

title: Fantastic to start your career
pros: Lots of support for beginning your career, initially a great salary. Several internal tools / courses to grow a variety of skills. Great co-workers, managers and social opportunities. Had great flexibility on work from home during my time, although there was a push back to the office before I left.
cons: Work life balance can be great from awful depending on the current project status. Unwilling to negotiate salary for the most part, after close to four years was disappointed with my overall compensation, Unsurprisingly as a consultant role the job can be unstable, likely also due to impacts from covid, but there were several rounds of layoffs in my practice throughout my time at Capgemini. I was laid off after the pipeline of work for the practice dried up throughout 2023, after an extremely taxing project. The layoff itself was with no official notice, and very quick (30 days) after rolling off my last project.

title: Insightful Reflections: My Journey at Capgemini â A Glassdoor Review
body: Insightful Reflections: My Journey at Capgemini â A Glassdoor Review
pros: - Work life balance - sponsoring Certifications - Paid PTO's - but depends on the grades within the company - Provides various online learning platforms to brush up the skills and learn new technologies - if you are laid off and the performance is good, they give severance. - benefits and everything is good. - had a good time working for this company.
cons: - lay off without prior notice. - When there's a freezing period(nov-jan) there will be no projects available, while on this time if you are on bench they try to push you in any project that is available regardless of the experience you have, they train us for the position in a very less time and wants us to perform well, then client wont be satisfying with our performance and rest is termination... - they call you directly and say you are laid off. - the structure of the management is not good, your supervisor, manager, performance reviewer all will be playing diff roles. - they take all our documents and say our H1 is registered, but not sure if it is really registered or not - no hike for 2 years since join date. - even when the client is okay to work from home, the management forces you to go to the office.

pros: Easy to get hired. People canât quit fast enough. Entire teams will quit within a few months of each other, and theyâll plug in anyone to take their place.
cons: Now that Iâve been out of Capgemini for years, I see even more clearly how truly awful the culture is. Itâs goes beyond just unpleasantness and being âtoxic.â I wish I had spoken to an employment lawyer during my time at Cap. The project leads are usually narcissists who have no qualms about lying to your face and making you responsible for their mistakes. Even worse things happen that you canât post in a Glassdoor review. Cap is a nightmare place to work. Get out as fast as you can.

title: Honest Review
pros: â¢ Decent salary straight out of college with avenues for promotion via NGT pathway â¢ Easy interview process â¢ My experience with management has overall been incredibly positive â¢ Company rewards self-starters. If you are gaining certifications and learning outside of your primary responsibilities, you will go far. â¢ Ability to move within company and departments â¢ Remote and hybrid options available depending on client and Capgemini offices are very nice â¢ Company heavily encourages you to gain certifications. This helps your career growth and is helpful if you want to move companies later on â¢ Better than many competitors such as TCS. I work with some employees from other consulting companies as part of my job on a regular basis and the difference in quality of work is noticeable.

title: It's not one company, its a group of companies
body: It's not one company, its a group of companies
pros: Good training opportunities Very supporting onsite team Extremally Poor offshore
cons: It is not one company, instead its a group of companies under one umbrella, operating in their own world and fighting among themselves. No one works for Capgemini, instead everyone works for their practice and only think of ways benefitting their practice. Practice is least bothered about delivery and the only focus is to put people on projects as soon as possible, even if that means snatching the position from other practice. There is no vision from the sr. leadership for the client and everything revolves around practice and improvement of the practice only. Horizontals are always fighting and do not care about verticals at all.

title: Ethical, biz-outcome-focused, customer-1st, tech-fueled, people-driven co for adults who love what they do
body: Ethical, biz-outcome-focused, customer-1st, tech-fueled, people-driven co for adults who love what they do
pros: - Ethical with leadership values driven from the very top - Business-outcome-focused - Customer-first - In a growing market, folks can get to play a variety of roles in fulfilment of their need for providing business and technology solutions - Abundant resources and partnerships for application of and training in different technologies, methodologies and domain expertise for business success - Diversity focused with upward trajectory path for diversity of thought - Multiple organizations and practices to learn from and adapt to - Synergy of architecture, engagement managment, business analytics, project management, agile methodologies, best practices in every aspect of the IT lifecyle and of cultures from 50 countires around the globe - Great benefits including of course medical, matching 401 k, leave and stock options - If you love adventure, you can find it here.
cons: - Company is for adults - people who can own delivery for the customer on behalf of the shareholders, wearing the owner hat. - Since the company provides solutions for the business world with a large focus on revenues from the US, a decline in the US market thats lasts longer than usual leads to job volatility - Your promotion may depend on the feedback of the immediate manager rather than on previous years and feedback received

title: Worst MSP to work for
pros: Above average pay for call center tech support role with benefits if you opt in. Company will place you in a temporary bench if/when client you're assigned to ends contract and awaiting new assignment, with pay during the time waiting, but the max time is subject to change at any time per company's decision. If the team your on is short staffed, it won't stay that way for long.
cons: They will claim there's possibility to move to another role/client after being with them for a year... But what they won't say is that if you want to advance to anything beyond call center that there is no growth opportunities unless you move to India and apply to join their offshore company/division instead. They will lie and tell you that you gain increase in pay upon positive annual review... What they don't tell you is that even if you do get a good annual review there's no guarantee you get pay raise... might as well expect it to simply not happen unless you get promoted instead... but to what when there's no roles available to get promoted to? Starting to see the problem? They do not stay consistent with any promises they make, including those made during interview process.

title: Ridiculous experience
pros: I was fully remote and got to travel while âworking.â Never had to use my PTO because no one was checking on me anyway.
cons: Not sure if others had this experience but I didnât learn a thing in my two years there. I was assigned to multiple BS projects. One included outlining Vegas parking lots in google maps. I never got to actually work on a project in my field which left me with no experience even after two full years of employment. I spent more time on coursera than on actual projects. It was an extremely isolating experience. I would schedule meetings with my manager in effort to find meaningful work and he wouldn't respond. Was finally laid off (on my birthday) which I consider a blessing in disguise.

title: Good and bad time in the company
body: Good and bad time in the company
pros: Capgemini is a great company when it comes to resources like Coursera, plural site, Udemy business, and etc.. They give you an endless amount of resources to help you get in a project. The benefits are also not too terrible
cons: Networking is not very easy in Capgemini, especially if you donât live at any of the on-site locations. They also do not provide relocation assistance very easily. If you are in bench for a long time, like four weeks, they will remove you, regardless of what your status is with getting a billable roll. Their layoff can also be instantly within a day

title: Great place to learn
pros: 1. People - great working atmosphere (P&C); little less great in Managed services 2. Good for early career orientation in IT - You can start with L1 SD job and develop IT knowledge and skills through academies organized by the employer and move to other IT-related roles - in SD operations management, project management, ITIL-related roles, and even strictly technical roles. You can take your career into your own hands but need to be stubborn and look for opportunities.
cons: 1. Training - the main focus is on technical training and certifications (Snow, Azure, Google Cloud, Microsoft, PowerAutomate etc) and it's difficult to get a budget for more business/leadership-oriented training. Also, training budgets per team are small (you can get one certification out of it per year unless you're in a strictly tech position). 2. Obligatory upsells - In project-based roles, everyone needs to sell more man-days to the customers. This is obligatory and your year-end appraisal depends on it.

title: Good company to work in, and stay in
body: Good company to work in, and stay in
pros: I love Capgemini because of the wonderful opportunities it has given me. It's not perfect, it does have a lot of problems, but it's the best place I've worked at in my entire career. There are much dirty politics which occur but 80% of the job is pretty ok. The way to survive is to keep your head down and do the job. The less attention you draw to yourself, the easier life will be here.
cons: a lot of politics, nepotism, favoritism, sexism, pretty much a lot of isms but considering most places I've worked in have been pretty bad, this is the best of the worst.
